author: @erfquake1
text: So a solar panel could generate a bit of electricity if (engineered correctly) one side were exposed to cooler night temps while the other were exposed to, say, the warmer temps inside the house itself. Couple that with the fact that solar cells aren't as efficient in very hot summer days. (they actually degrade a bit) One concludes that for max efficiency solar cell design should integrate the thermal properties of, well, the property itself. If the panels were mounted on thick masonry and/or passive fluid radiator that acted as a big, slow heat sink, they might work better in both temperature scenarios? Fascinating.

replies.author: @markhathaway9456
replies.text: Light is spread and a typical PV array is huge to be effective. Heat from sunlight is also spread, though certain latitudes get more than others. Wind relates a lot to heat, but some places get more because of flow off the oceans. Heat however, is also a byproduct of industrial processes and that can be used. Heat also causes water to evaporate, rise, and then fall again as rain. Water drainage thus becomes gravity power and small turbines can capture some of that. Larger turbines are too tied to the water sources to be perfect everywhere (despite being super efficient). So, it may not be created or destroyed, but capturing it requires some ingenuity and converting it to electricity is also a bit tricky.

author: @anvilhead0
text: I sometimes think green energy is being overthought. I visited Finch’s Foundry at Sticklepath, near Okehampton, Devon last weekend. Not a foundry at all, but a forge that used to produce around 400 edge tools a day of various types. All powered by water wheels. Of course you have to have a reliable supply, but of course what you use is returned to the river, and could be used by hundreds further along. No pollution, virtually no noise. I don’t think we’ve advanced at all in the last two centuries.

replies.author: @thegrayinthefield8764
replies.text: Because we haven't actually advanced in any meaningful ways. When you start digging through the technology we had (as well as what we were actually capable of), stalled century stops being a "conspiracy theory" and starts becoming more and more plausible.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Thanks for the video... my big issue with the night time solar version is that the media really went overboard with excitement. As you mention, In reality the power output is minuscule at night... 50 mW / square meter or less. 
However, you mentioned heat sinks! So, yes, I think what is more important is the idea of a low cost heat sink ... If you recall, there are "white paints" that can act to lower the temperature of a surface. If you paint the back of the solar cell with this paint, you will improve the efficiency of the cell when it matters most... during sun light.
For example: Assume a solar panel produces 400 watts and has a temperature coefficient of -0.3% ... If the temperature is 5 degrees C warmer, we lose (0.3/100) * 5 = ~ 6 watts of power.
That 6 watts is fairly significant vs 50mW. So, just by cooling the panel 5 degrees C in daylight, we gain much more than night time production by ~ 100x more production in power vs the night production.

replies.text: It has a way to go that's for sure and i do remember the white paint, two versons i think - one uses densly packed calcium carbonate and the other barium sulfate - but they are trialling production versions so it should be interesting lol

author: @EliotTruelove
text:
Peltier devices, stirling engines, and thermophotovoltaics are fascinating to me. 

The fact that for centuries now we have used heat to produce mechanical energy through steam turbines seems so archaic to me.

Direct heat to electricity in a workabke way us the biggest leap in energy production possible that many people would do well to tackle or improve upon with material sciences. If we ever crack fusion, thermophotovoltaic plates in the chamber, along with som traditional photovoltaics with the expekled light, would be the best way to capture that energy in a simple way. To heat water or other fluids to turn a turbine can accomplish a cooling loop, and can be made more efficient than TPV panels, but is extremely complex, and extreme complexity is very expensive. 

If Fusion ever produces so much more than its inputs (accounting for the energy used to run the inputs in the first place and not just the enregy actually added) then TPV could directly use that energy and keave all the complexity to the reaction devices, whehter it be lasers, magnetic field generstors etc. As of now the piping and eputing of fluid around the device would interfere with much of the other mechanisms. TPV paneles theoretically would just need leads in and out.

author: @benmcreynolds8581
text: It's crazy how we think about light & dark but we couldn't have the universe without light. Light is connected to energy, heat, magnetism, electrostatic charge. All these things create the building blocks to form nearly everything in our universe. It really shows the important impact of even the Slightest differences in a given environment. Temperature, density, pressure, charge, etc. Etc.

replies.author: @filonin2
replies.text: The universe already existed without light. It was called the Cosmic Dark Ages, the time from 380,000 years after the Big Bang until about 1 billion years after when the first stars formed. It was dark for hundreds of millions of years.

author: @szogun1987
text:
This episode lead me to following though experiment 
Let's assume following setup:
There is a water heat(or cold) buffer
Durring day heat pump uses electricity from PV to cool down house and heat up  water.
After sunset water from buffer is pumped to panels covered with IR reflective paint and LDPE, and uses Peltier's to harvest some of energy.
Would Peltier's even power pumps? If so how much would I be able to power with remaining power. How would look in comparison to dumping heat from heat pump to atmosphere and collecting some energy from PV in batteries.

replies.author: @UncleKennysPlace
replies.text: Peltier devices are anything but efficient.

replies.author: @johnlambert7405
replies.text: I think the first part of your experiment is essentially a hybrid solar PV-T panel. Look into those and see how you feel they work. I got really excited when Robert began the video because I plan on using some PV-Ts on a tracker in a temperate climate. However, when I got to the rather paltry energy density figures my enthusiasm waned. I think there are probably applications where the scheme you are describing would work. I just think that in areas where cooling or heating is required it makes more sense to keep the either hot or cold water and exchange their BTUs before the inlet side of a heat pump directly. So cool how this video got everyone's gears turning!

replies.author: @szogun1987
replies.text:
@johnlambert7405 My idea does not include collecting heat from sunlight. But it seems like a nice extension.
Idea was that it would be device for remote location on deserts. They need cold place during day, they are poor so PV and heat pump has to as cheap/as small as possible. Low temperature and hight specific heat capacity of water would increase heat pumps efficiency. If Peltier's would be effective enough to provide energy for pumps and lights on evenings it would be a great win - no need to provide batteries.

author: @Forensic1Man
text: Hi!  Another very nice video.  Have you heard of "Perovskite Solar Cells"?  The material appears simple enough, very high efficiency, and cost efficient enough that we should investigate this material for DIY home solar cells!  What do you think? Could you make some perovskite material?

replies.author: @Ammoniummetavanadate
replies.text:
Been under investigation since the 80s with a break until say 2012, U of T has a lab going hard on this, Ted Sargent if memory serves.

I remember the shift back to perovskites vividly as I was working on dye sensitized solar at the time (mainly etching titanium dioxide nano arrays) and that whole field died when perovskites hit the mainstream again.

Doing it at home is possible but the chemicals are a touch on the nasty side. I wouldn't want a layman doing it simply from disposal concerns.

replies.author: @Forensic1Man
replies.text: Yes, the lead in the perovskite cells could be disposal and reagent problem for most. But not for you!!@Ammoniummetavanadate

replies.author: @john_blues
replies.text: A perovskite solar cell is not easy to manufacture(for DIY). And they degrade much quicker.
